name: autoray

metadata.name: autoray
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License

documentation:
![autoray-header](https://github.com/jcmgray/autoray/assets/8982598/c5cb89bf-cc16-4345-8796-e0bd98dc2a15)

[![tests](https://github.com/jcmgray/autoray/actions/workflows/tests.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/jcmgray/autoray/actions/workflows/tests.yml)
[![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/jcmgray/autoray/branch/main/graph/badge.svg?token=Q5evNiuT9S)](https://codecov.io/gh/jcmgray/autoray)
[![Codacy Badge](https://app.codacy.com/project/badge/Grade/ba896d74c4954dd58da01df30c7bf326)](https://app.codacy.com/gh/jcmgray/autoray/dashboard?utm_source=gh&utm_medium=referral&utm_content=&utm_campaign=Badge_grade)
[![Docs](https://readthedocs.org/projects/autoray/badge/?version=latest)](https://autoray.readthedocs.io)
[![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/autoray?color=teal)](https://pypi.org/project/autoray/)
[![Anaconda-Server Badge](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/autoray/badges/version.svg)](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/autoray)

[`autoray`](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest) is a lightweight python AUTOmatic-arRAY library for
abstracting your tensor operations. Primarily it provides an
[*automatic* dispatch mechanism](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/automatic_dispatch.html#)
that means you can write backend agnostic code that works for:

* [numpy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy)
* [pytorch](https://pytorch.org/)
* [jax](https://github.com/google/jax)
* [cupy](https://github.com/cupy/cupy)
* [dask](https://github.com/dask/dask)
* [autograd](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd)
* [tensorflow](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow)
* [sparse](https://sparse.pydata.org/)
* [mars](https://github.com/mars-project/mars)
* ... and indeed **any** library that provides a numpy-*ish* api, even if it
  knows nothing about `autoray`.

Beyond that, abstracting the array interface allows you to:

* *swap [custom versions of functions](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/automatic_dispatch.html#functions)
  for specific backends*
* *trace through computations [lazily](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/lazy_computation.html) without actually
  running them*
* *automatically [share intermediates and fold constants](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/lazy_computation.html#sharing-intermediates)
  in computations*
* *compile functions with a [unified interface](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/compilation.html) for different
  backends*


## Basic usage

The main function of `autoray` is
[`do`](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/autoray/autoray/index.html#autoray.autoray.do),
which takes a function
name followed by `*args` and `**kwargs`, and automatically looks up (and
caches) the correct function to match the equivalent numpy call:

```python
from autoray as ar

def noised_svd(x):
    # automatic dispatch based on supplied array
    U, s, VH = ar.do('linalg.svd', x)

    # automatic dispatch based on different array
    sn = s + 0.1 * ar.do('random.normal', size=ar.shape(s), like=s)

    # automatic dispatch for multiple arrays for certain functions
    return ar.do('einsum', 'ij,j,jk->ik', U, sn, VH)

# explicit backend given by string
x = ar.do('random.uniform', size=(100, 100), like="torch")

# this function now works for any backend
y = noised_svd(x)

# explicit inference of backend from array
ar.infer_backend(y)
# 'torch'
```

If you don't like the explicit `do` syntax, or simply want a
drop-in replacement for existing code, you can also import the `autoray.numpy`
module:

```python
from autoray import numpy as np

# set a temporary default backend
with ar.backend_like('cupy'):
    z = np.ones((3, 4), dtype='float32')

np.exp(z)
# array([[2.7182817, 2.7182817, 2.7182817, 2.7182817],
#        [2.7182817, 2.7182817, 2.7182817, 2.7182817],
#        [2.7182817, 2.7182817, 2.7182817, 2.7182817]], dtype=float32)
```

Custom backends and functions can be dynamically registered with:

* [`register_backend`](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/autoray/autoray/index.html#autoray.autoray.register_backend)
* [`register_function`](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/autoray/autoray/index.html#autoray.autoray.register_function)

The main documentation is available at [autoray.readthedocs.io](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).

modules.name: autoray.experimental.complexity_tracing
documentation:
Functionality for tracing through an autoray.lazy computation and estimating
the cost and scaling.

In the following there are `cost_*` functions that estimate the total cost
of a given operation, including sub-leading factors. There are also
`cost_scaling_*` functions that only consider the leading factor of the cost,
so that we can prime number decompose it and extract the scaling.

functions.name: autoray.experimental.complexity_tracing.cost_scaling_linalg
documentation:
Here we only care about the leading factor of the cost, which we need to
preserve so that we can prime number decompose it.

modules.name: autoray.lazy.linalg
documentation: TODO: lstsq, pinv, eigvals, eigvalsh

functions.name: autoray.compiler.autojit
documentation:
Just-in-time compile an `autoray` function, automatically choosing
the backend based on the input arrays, or via keyword argument.

The backend used to do the compilation can be set in three ways:

> 1. Automatically based on the arrays the function is called with,
>    i.e. `cfn(*torch_arrays)` will use `torch.jit.trace`.
> 2. In this wrapper, `@autojit(backend='jax')`, to provide a
>    specific default instead.
> 3. When you call the function `cfn(*arrays, backend='torch')` to
>    override on a per-call basis.

If the arrays supplied are of a different backend type to the compiler,
then the returned array will also be converted back, i.e.
`cfn(*numpy_arrays, backend='tensorflow')` will return a `numpy` array.

The `'python'` backend simply extracts and unravels all the `do` calls
into a code object using `compile` which is then run with `exec`.
This makes use of shared intermediates and constant folding, strips
away any python scaffoliding, and is compatible with any library, but the
resulting function is not 'low-level' in the same way as the other
backends.

# Parameters

fn

: The autoray function to compile.

backend

: If set, use this as the default backend.

compiler_opts

: Dict of dicts when you can supply options for each compiler backend
  separately, e.g.:
  `@autojit(compiler_opts={'tensorflow': {'jit_compile': True}})`.

# Returns

cfn

: The function with auto compilation.

classes.name: autoray.compiler.CompilePython

methods.name: autoray.compiler.CompilePython.setup
documentation:
Convert the example arrays to lazy variables and trace them through
the function.

methods.name: autoray.compiler.CompilePython.__call__
documentation: If necessary, build, then call the compiled function.

A simple compiler that unravels all autoray calls, optionally sharing
intermediates and folding constants, converts this to a code object using
`compile`, then executes this using `exec`.

# Parameters

fn

: Function to compile - should have signature
  `fn(*args, **kwargs) -> array`, with `args` and `kwargs` any
  nested combination of `tuple`, `list` and `dict` objects
  containing arrays (or other constant arguments), and perform array
  operations on these using `autoray.do`.

fold_constants

: Whether to fold all constant array operations into the graph, which
  might increase memory usage.

share_intermediates

: Whether to cache all computational nodes during the trace, so that any
  shared intermediate results can be identified.

classes.name: autoray.compiler.AutoCompiled
documentation:
Just in time compile a `autoray.do` using function. See the main
wrapper `autojit`.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.draw.plot_graph
documentation: Plot the computational graph of this `LazyArray`.
def get_nice_pos():
"""Get a nice layout for a graph.
# compute a layout for the graph
if initial_layout == "layers":
for layer, nodes in enumerate(nx.topological_generations(G)):
for node in nodes:
G.nodes[node]["layer"] = layer
layout_opts.setdefault("subset_key", "layer")
layout_opts.setdefault("align", "vertical")
pos = nx.multipartite_layout(G, **layout_opts)
if layout_opts["align"] == "horizontal":
dag_spread = 1 / dag_spread
for k, (x, y) in pos.items():
pos[k] = (x, dag_spread * y)
else:
if initial_layout == "spiral":
layout_opts.setdefault("equidistant", True)
pos = getattr(nx, initial_layout + "_layout")(G, **layout_opts)
# further spring based refinement
if iterations:
pos = nx.layout.spring_layout(G, pos=pos, k=k, iterations=iterations)

Plot the memory footprint throughout this computation.

figsize

color

: Color of the line.

alpha

ax

: Axes to plot on, will be created if not provided.

return_fig

modules.name: autoray.lazy.core
documentation: Core lazy array functionality.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.is_lazy_array
documentation: Check if `x` is a lazy array.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.to_queue
documentation:
Parse a pytree of lazy arrays into a queue of nodes, sorted by depth.
This is useful for traversing the computational graph of multiple outputs
in topological order.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.descend
documentation:
Generate each unique computational node. Use `ascend` if you need to
visit children before parents.

# Parameters

lz

: The output node(s) of the computational graph to descend.

# Yields

LazyArray

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.ascend
documentation:
Generate each unique computational node, from leaves to root. I.e. a
topological ordering of the computational graph. Moreover, the nodes
are visited 'deepest first'.

Parameters
----------
lz : pytree of LazyArray
    The output node(s) of the computational graph to ascend to.

Yields
------
LazyArray

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.compute
documentation:
Compute the value of one or more lazy arrays. All nodes that are
computed clear any references to their function, arguments and
dependencies, and store the result in their `_data` attribute.

# Parameters

lz

: The output node(s) of the computational graph to compute.

# Returns

array or tuple of array

: The computed value(s) of the lazy array(s).

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.compute_constants
documentation:
Fold constant arrays - everything not dependent on `variables` -
into the graph.

# Parameters

lz

: The output node(s) of the computational graph.

variables

: Nodes that should be treated as variable. I.e. any descendants will
  not be folded into the graph.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.get_source
documentation:
Write the source code of an unravelled version of the computational
graph, injecting required runtime objects into `params`.

# Parameters

lz

: The output node(s) of the computational graph to write the source code
  for. Their corresponding label is `f"x{id(node)}"` in the
  source code.

# Returns

str

: The source code of the computational graph, suitable for `exec`.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.find_lazy
documentation: Recursively search for `LazyArray` instances in pytrees.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.maybe_materialize
documentation: Recursively evaluate LazyArray instances in tuples, lists and dicts.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.stringify_larray

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.stringify

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.currently_sharing
documentation: Check if we are currently sharing a cache -- thread specific.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.get_sharing_cache
documentation: Return the most recent sharing cache -- thread specific.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.shared_intermediates
documentation:
Context in which intermediate results are shared.

Note that intermediate LazyArray instances (which can reference actual
data) will not be garbage collected until
1. this context exits, and
2. the yielded cache is garbage collected (if it was captured).

Parameters
----------
cache : dict
    If specified, a user-stored dict in which intermediate lazy arrays will
    be stored. This can be used to interleave sharing contexts.

Returns
-------
cache : dict
    A dictionary in which sharing results are stored. If ignored,
    sharing results will be garbage collected when this context is
    exited. This dict can be passed to another context to resume
    sharing.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.lazy_cache
documentation:
Decorator to mark a function as being lazy cacheable.

# Parameters

fn_name

: The name to use for the function in the cache.

hasher

: A function with signature `hasher(fn_name, *args, **kwargs)` that
  returns a hashable key for the cache. If not specified, the default
  is to use `hash_args_kwargs`.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.Variable
documentation:
Create a `LazyArray` from a shape only, representing a leaf node
in the computational graph. It can only act as a placeholder for data.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.array
documentation:
Create a `LazyArray` from an input array, representing a leaf node
in the computational graph.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core._get_py_shape
documentation: Infer the shape of a possibly nested list/tuple object.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.empty
documentation: Lazy creation of an empty array with a given shape.
# XXX: still missing
allclose
dot, vdot, inner, outer
pad, eye
squeeze, expand_dims
to_numpy

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.zeros
documentation: Lazy creation of an array filled with zeros with a given shape.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.ones
documentation: Lazy creation of an array filled with ones with a given shape.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.eye
documentation: Lazy creation of the identity matrix of size N.

functions.name: autoray.lazy.core.identity
documentation: Lazy creation of the identity matrix of size n.

classes.name: autoray.lazy.core.Function

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.Function.print_source
documentation: Print the source code of the compiled function.

Get a compiled (by python `compile`), function that performs the
computational graph corresponding to `inputs` -> `outputs`. The
signature of the function is `func(input_arrays) -> output_arrays`. As an
intermediate step, the computational graph is traced to a flattened source
code string.

# Parameters

inputs

: The input node(s) of the computational graph.

outputs

: The output node(s) of the computational graph.

fold_constants

: If True, fold constant arrays (those with no dependence on `inputs`)
  into the graph ahead of compile.

# See Also

get_source, compute

classes.name: autoray.lazy.core.Placeholder
documentation: A singleton object to use as a placeholder in a LazyArray.

classes.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.from_data
documentation: Create a new `LazyArray` directly from a concrete array.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.from_shape
documentation: Create a new `LazyArray` with a given shape.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.to
documentation:
Create a new `LazyArray`, by default propagating backend, shape,
and deps from the the current LazyArray.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray._materialize
documentation:
Recursively compute all required args and kwargs for this node
before computing itself and dereferencing dependencies. Note using this
to materialize a large computation from scratch should be avoided due
to the recursion limit, use `x.compute()` instead.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.compute
documentation:
Compute the value of this lazy array, clearing any references to the
function, arguments and dependencies, and storing the result in the
`_data` attribute as well as returning it.

Unlike `self._materialize()` this avoids deep recursion.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.as_string
documentation: Create a string which evaluates to the lazy array creation.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.get_function
documentation:
Get a compiled function that computes `fn(arrays)`, with `fn`
describing the computational graph of this `LazyArray` and `arrays`
corresponding to the downstream `LazyArray` nodes `variables`.

# Parameters

variables

: Input nodes whose data can change between calls.

fold_constants

: Compute all intermediates which do not depend on `variables`
  prior to compilation.

# Returns

fn

: Function with signature `fn(arrays)`.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.show
documentation: Show the computational graph as a nested directory structure.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.history_num_nodes
documentation:
Return the number of unique computational nodes in the history of
this `LazyArray`.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.history_max_size
documentation: Get the largest single tensor size appearing in this computation.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.history_size_footprint
documentation:
Get the combined size of intermediates at each step of the
computation. Note this assumes that intermediates are immediately
garbage collected when they are no longer required.

# Parameters

include_inputs

: Whether to include the size of the inputs in the computation. If
  `True` It is assumed they can be garbage collected once used but
  are all present at the beginning of the computation.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.history_peak_size
documentation:
Get the peak combined intermediate size of this computation.

# Parameters

include_inputs

: Whether to include the size of the inputs in the computation. If
  `True` It is assumed they can be garbage collected once used but
  are all present at the beginning of the computation.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.history_total_size
documentation:
The the total size of all unique arrays in the computational graph,
possibly relevant e.g. for back-propagation algorithms.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.history_stats
documentation:
Compute aggregate statistics about the computational graph.

Parameters
----------
fn : callable or str
    Function to apply to each node in the computational graph. If a
    string, one of 'count', 'sizein', 'sizeout' can be used to count
    the number of nodes, the total size of the inputs, or the total
    size of each output respectively.

Returns
-------
stats : dict
    Dictionary mapping function names to the aggregate statistics.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.history_fn_frequencies
documentation:
Get a dictionary mapping function names to the number of times they
are used in the computational graph.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.to_nx_digraph
documentation: Convert this `LazyArray` into a `networkx.DiGraph`.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.fn
documentation: The function to use to compute this array.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.fn_name
documentation: The name of the function to use to compute this array.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.args
documentation:
The positional arguments to the function to use to compute this
array.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.kwargs
documentation:
The keyword arguments to the function to use to compute this
array.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.deps
documentation:
A tuple of the dependencies, other LazyArray instances, of this
array.

methods.name: autoray.lazy.core.LazyArray.depth
documentation: The maximum distance to any input array in the computational graph.

A lazy array representing a node in a computational graph.

# Parameters

backend

: The backend of the array were it to be computed. This can be `None`
  but this may cause problems when propagating information about which
  functions to import to child nodes.

fn

: The function to call to compute the array, presumable imported from
  `backend`. This can be `None` if the array already has data (e.g.
  is an input).

args

: The positional arguments to pass to `fn`, which might be
  `LazyArray` instances.

kwargs

: The keyword arguments to pass to `fn`, which might be
  `LazyArray` instances.

shape

: The shape of the array that `fn(*args, **kwargs)` will return, or
  the shape of the array that `data` has.

deps

: The `LazyArray` instances that `fn(*args, **kwargs)` depends on.
  If not specified, these will be automatically found from `args` and
  `kwargs`, specifying them manually is slightly more efficient.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.do
documentation:
Do function named `fn` on `(*args, **kwargs)`, peforming single
dispatch to retrieve `fn` based on whichever library defines the class of
the `args[0]`, or the `like` keyword argument if specified.

# Examples

Works on numpy arrays:

> ```pycon
> >>> import numpy as np
> >>> x_np = np.random.uniform(size=[5])
> >>> y_np = do('sqrt', x_np)
> >>> y_np
> array([0.32464973, 0.90379787, 0.85037325, 0.88729814, 0.46768083])
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> type(y_np)
> numpy.ndarray
> ```

Works on cupy arrays:

> ```pycon
> >>> import cupy as cp
> >>> x_cp = cp.random.uniform(size=[5])
> >>> y_cp = do('sqrt', x_cp)
> >>> y_cp
> array([0.44541656, 0.88713113, 0.92626237, 0.64080557, 0.69620767])
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> type(y_cp)
> cupy.core.core.ndarray
> ```

Works on tensorflow arrays:

> ```pycon
> >>> import tensorflow as tf
> >>> x_tf = tf.random.uniform(shape=[5])
> >>> y_tf = do('sqrt', x_tf)
> >>> y_tf
> <tf.Tensor 'Sqrt_1:0' shape=(5,) dtype=float32>
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> type(y_tf)
> tensorflow.python.framework.ops.Tensor
> ```

You get the idea.

For functions that don't dispatch on the first argument you can use the
`like` keyword:

> ```pycon
> >>> do('eye', 3, like=x_tf)
> <tf.Tensor: id=91, shape=(3, 3), dtype=float32>
> ```

functions.name: autoray.autoray._default_infer_from_sig
documentation:
This is the default backend dispatcher, used if no global backend has
been set. Hot swapping this function out as below avoids having to check
manually for a global backend or worse, a thread aware global backend, on
every call to `do`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.get_backend
documentation:
Return the universally set backend, if any.

Parameters
----------
get_globally : {"auto", False, True}, optional
    Which backend to return:

    - True: return the globally set backend, if any.
    - False: return the backend set for the current thread, if any.
    - "auto": return the globally set backend, if this thread is the thread
      that imported autoray. Otherwise return the backend set for the
      current thread, if any.

Returns
-------
backend : str or None
    The name of the backend, or None if no backend is set.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.set_backend
documentation:
Set a default global backend. The argument `like` can be an explicit
backend name or an `array`.

# Parameters

like

: The backend to set. If an array, the backend of the array's class will
  be set.

set_globally

: Whether to set the backend globally or for the current thread:

  - True: set the backend globally.
  - False: set the backend for the current thread.
  - "auto": set the backend globally if this thread is the thread that
    imported autoray. Otherwise set the backend for the current thread.

  Only one thread should ever call this function with
  `set_globally=True`, (by default this is importing thread).

functions.name: autoray.autoray.backend_like
documentation:
Context manager for setting a default backend. The argument `like` can
be an explicit backend name or an `array` to infer it from.

# Parameters

like

: The backend to set. If an array, the backend of the array's class will
  be set.

set_globally

: Whether to set the backend globally or for the current thread:

  - True: set the backend globally.
  - False: set the backend for the current thread.
  - "auto": set the backend globally if this thread is the thread that
    imported autoray. Otherwise set the backend for the current thread.

  Only one thread should ever call this function with
  `set_globally=True`, (by default this is importing thread).

functions.name: autoray.autoray.register_backend
documentation:
Register the name (and by default the module or submodule) of a custom
array class.

Parameters
----------
cls : type
    The array class itself.
name : str
    The name of the backend that should be used for this class. By default
    this wil be assumed to be the location of the relevant functions for
    this class, but this can be overridden.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.infer_backend
documentation:
Get the name of the library that defined the class of `array` - unless
`array` is directly a subclass of `numpy.ndarray`, in which case assume
`numpy` is the desired backend.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.infer_backend_multi
documentation:
Infer which backend should be used for a function that takes multiple
arguments. This assigns a priority to each backend, and returns the backend
with the highest priority. By default, the priority is:

- `builtins`: -2
- `numpy`: -1
- other backends: 0
- `autoray.lazy`: 1

I.e. when mixing with `numpy`, other array libraries are preferred, when
mixing with `autoray.lazy`, `autoray.lazy` is preferred. This has quite
low overhead due to caching.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.register_creation_routine
documentation:
Register a function that creates a new array, with `dtype` and possibly
`device` kwargs, that should be inferred from the like argument. This is
not necessary for array creation routines that don't accept either.

Parameters
----------
backend : str
    The backend to register the function for.
fn : str
    The name of the function to register.
inject_dtype : bool, optional
    Whether to inject a `dtype` argument based on the `like` argument.
inject_device : bool, optional
    Whether to inject a `device` argument based on the `like` argument.

functions.name: autoray.autoray._choose_backend
documentation:
Private function to choose a backend based on function name and
signature, which passes args and kwargs by reference for performance and
also to allow injection of dtype and device arguments for array creation
routines.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.choose_backend
documentation:
Choose a backend based on function name, arguments, and the `like`
keyword argument. The default, if `like` is not specified, is to infer
the backend from the function call, the default of which is simply to use
the first argument, if no custom dispatcher is found. Otherwise the
backend is chosen based on the `like` argument - which can be an explicit
backend name or an arbitrary object.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.get_lib_fn
documentation:
Cached retrieval of correct function for backend, all the logic for
finding the correct funtion only runs the first time.

Parameters
----------
backend : str
    The module defining the array class to dispatch on.
fn : str
    The function to retrieve.

Returns
-------
callable

functions.name: autoray.autoray.register_function
documentation:
Directly provide your own function.

# Parameters

backend

: The name of the backend to register the function for.

name

: Name of the function, e.g. `'sum'` or `'linalg.svd'`.

fn

: The function to register.

wrap

: Whether to wrap the old function like `fn(old_fn)` rather than
  directly supply the entire new function.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.tree_register_container
documentation:
Register a new container type for use with `tree_map` and
`tree_apply`.

# Parameters

cls

: The container type to register.

mapper

: A function that takes `f`, `tree` and `is_leaf` and returns a new
  tree of type `cls` with `f` applied to all leaves.

applier

: A function that takes `f`, `tree` and `is_leaf` and applies `f`
  to all leaves in `tree`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.is_not_container
documentation:
The default function to determine if an object is a leaf. This simply
checks if the object is an instance of any of the registered container
types.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.is_array
documentation: An alternative leaf tester for addressing only arrays within trees.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.tree_map
documentation:
Map `f` over all leaves in `tree`, returning a new pytree.

# Parameters

f

: A function to apply to all leaves in `tree`.

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, `f` is only applied
  to objects for which `is_leaf(x)` returns `True`.

# Returns

pytree

functions.name: autoray.autoray.tree_iter
documentation:
Iterate over all leaves in `tree`.

# Parameters

f

: A function to apply to all leaves in `tree`.

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, `f` is only applied
  to objects for which `is_leaf(x)` returns `True`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.tree_apply
documentation:
Apply `f` to all leaves in `tree`, no new pytree is built.

# Parameters

f

: A function to apply to all leaves in `tree`.

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, `f` is only applied
  to objects for which `is_leaf(x)` returns `True`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.tree_flatten
documentation:
Flatten `tree` into a list of leaves.

# Parameters

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, only objects for which
  `is_leaf(x)` returns `True` are returned in the flattened list.

get_ref

: If `True`, a reference tree is also returned which can be used to
  reconstruct the original tree from a flattened list.

# Returns

objs

: The flattened list of leaf objects.

(ref_tree)

: If `get_ref` is `True`, a reference tree, with leaves of `Leaf`,
  is returned which can be used to reconstruct the original tree.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.tree_unflatten
documentation:
Unflatten `objs` into a pytree of the same structure as `tree`.

# Parameters

objs

: A sequence of objects to be unflattened into a pytree.

tree

: A nested sequence of tuples, lists, dicts and other objects, the objs
  will be inserted into a new pytree of the same structure.

is_leaf

: A function to determine if an object is a leaf, only objects for which
  `is_leaf(x)` returns `True` will have the next item from `objs`
  inserted. By default checks for the `Leaf` object inserted by
  `tree_flatten(..., get_ref=True)`.

# Returns

pytree

functions.name: autoray.autoray.compose
documentation:
Take a function consisting of multiple `autoray.do` calls and compose
it into a new, single, named function, registered with `autoray.do`.

This creates a default implementation of this function for each new backend
encountered without explicitly having to write each out, but also allows
for specific implementations to be overridden for specific backends.

If the function takes a `backend` argument, it will be supplied with the
backend name, to save having to re-choose the backend.

Specific implementations can be provided by calling the `register` method
of the composed function, or it can itself be used like a decorator:

```
@compose
def foo(x):
    ...

@foo.register("numpy")
@numba.njit
def foo_numba(x):
    ...
```

# Parameters

fn

: The funtion to compose, and its default implementation.

name

: The name of the composed function. If not provided, the name of the
  function will be used.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.shape
documentation:
Get the shape of an array as a tuple of int. This should be preferred
to calling `x.shape` directly, as it:

    1. Allows customization (e.g. for torch and aesara which return
       different types for shape - use `@shape.register(backend)` to
       customize the behavior from this default implementation).
    2. Can be used on nested lists and tuples, without calling numpy.

Parameters
----------
x : array_like
    The array to get the shape of. It can be an arbitrary nested list or
    tuple of arrays and scalars, but is assumed not to be ragged.

Returns
-------
shape : tuple of int
    The size of each dimension of the array.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.ndim
documentation:
Get the number of dimensions of an array. This should be preferred to
calling `x.ndim`, since not all backends implement that, and it can also be
called on nested lists and tuples.

Parameters
----------
x : array_like
    The array to get the number of dimensions of. It can be an arbitrary
    nested list or tuple of arrays and scalars.

Returns
-------
ndim : int

functions.name: autoray.autoray.size
documentation:
Get the size, or number of elements, of an array. This should be
preferred to calling `x.size`, since not all backends implement that, and
it can also be called on nested lists and tuples.

Parameters
----------
x : array_like
    The array to get the size of. It can be an arbitrary nested list or
    tuple of arrays and scalars.

Returns
-------
size : int

functions.name: autoray.autoray.conj
documentation: Array conjugate.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.transpose
documentation: Array transpose.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.dag
documentation: Array Hermitian transpose.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.real
documentation: Array real part.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.imag
documentation: Array imaginary part.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.reshape
documentation: Array reshaped.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.to_backend_dtype
documentation: Turn string specifier `dtype_name` into dtype of backend `like`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.get_dtype_name
documentation: Find string specifier `dtype_name` of array `x`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.get_common_dtype
documentation: Compute the minimal dtype sufficient for `arrays`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.astype
documentation: Cast array as type `dtype_name` - tries `x.astype` first.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.to_numpy
documentation: Get a numpy version of array `x`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.with_dtype_wrapper
documentation:
Add ability to handle `dtype` keyword.
If not None, `dtype` should be specified as a string, otherwise conversion
will happen regardless.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.translate_wrapper
documentation:
Wrap a function to match the api of another according to a translation.
The `translator` entries in the form of an ordered dict should have
entries like:

> (desired_kwarg: (backend_kwarg, default_value))

with the order defining the args of the function.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.wrap_args_kwargs_from_raw
documentation:
Take a function with signature `(*args, **kwargs)` and wrap it to
accept a single tuple of args and a dict of kwargs.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.register_dispatch
documentation:
Register a new dispatcher, a function that takes the arguments and
keyword arguments of a function and returns the backend to use, when the
backend is not explicitly given.

This is useful in case the backend to be used by a function cannot be
inferred from the first argument.

# Parameters

fun

: The name of the function to register the dispatcher for.

dispatcher

: The dispatcher function to use. This should take the arguments and
  keyword arguments of the function and return the backend to use.

raw_signature

: The `dispatcher` has signature `(*args, **kwargs)` if `True`,
  otherwise it has signature `(args, kwargs)`.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.default_dispatcher
documentation: Try to infer backend from first argument passed to function.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.join_array_dispatcher
documentation: Dispatcher for functions where first argument is a sequence.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.einsum_dispatcher
documentation:
Dispatcher for handling einsum.

einsum can be called with a str equation as the first argument, or with
'interleaved' inputs. This dispatcher handles both cases and also takes
into account all arrays.

functions.name: autoray.autoray.binary_dispatcher
documentation:
There are cases when we want to take into account both backends of two
arguments, e.g. a lazy variable and a constant array.

functions.name: autoray.autoray._get_mimic_function_or_attribute
documentation: TODO: register other binary functions?
--------------- object to act as drop-in replace for numpy ---------------- #

classes.name: autoray.autoray.Leaf
documentation:
A singleton object to use as a placeholder in a pytree, for
unflattening.

classes.name: autoray.autoray.Composed

methods.name: autoray.autoray.Composed.register
documentation: Register a different implementation for `backend`.

methods.name: autoray.autoray.Composed.make_function
documentation: Make a new function for the specific `backend`.

Compose an `autoray.do` using function. See the main wrapper
`compose`.

classes.name: autoray.autoray.NumpyMimic
documentation: A class to mimic the syntax of using `numpy` directly.
